coredata.dc:title: Exclusive nitrate to ammonia conversion via boron-doped carbon dots induced surface Lewis acid sites
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Xingmei
Yu, Jingkun
Cai, Jinmeng
Zhang, Qinghua
Yang, Siwei
Gu, Lin
Waterhouse, Geoffrey I.N.
Zang, Shuang-Quan
Yang, Bai
Lu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: Electrocatalytic nitrate reduction represents a very promising route for ammonia synthesis under ambient conditions. However, for most nitrate-to-ammonia electrocatalysts developed to date, the Faradaic efficiency (FE) to ammonia is modest due to the competitive hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Here, we report that the decoration of vertically aligned NiCo2O4 pillars with boron-doped carbon dots (BCDs) enables electrocatalytic nitrate reduction to ammonia with ∼100% FE. A BCDs/NiCo2O4/carbon cloth cathode delivers a NH3 production rate of 173.9 μmol h−1 cm−2 at −0.55 V versus reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). This state-of-the-art performance is linked to the BCDs, which provide an abundance of Lewis acid sites on the electrocatalyst surface for the adsorption of nitrate ions (simultaneously suppressing the competitive HER). This work reveals that highly selective NH3 electrosynthesis can be achieved under ambient conditions by regulating the surface acidity/basicity properties of metal-oxide-based catalysts, paving the way toward more sustainable NH3-production technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: electrocatalysis
ammonia synthesis
nitrate reduction
Lewis acid sites
boron-doped carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC
(A) Schematic illustration of the preparation of the BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC nanowire arrays.
(B and C) SEM images of the precursor catalyst and BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC.
(D) STEM image and corresponding element mapping images of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC.
(E–G) AC-HAADF-STEM images of (E) NiCo2O4/CC and (F and G) BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC.
(H and I) Partial enlargement of the red dotted areas in (F) and (G), respectively.
Characterization and surface properties of the catalysts
(A) XRD patterns of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC, NiCo2O4/CC, and CC.
(B) Raman spectra of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC, NiCo2O4/CC, and CC.
(C–F) XPS spectra of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC and NiCo2O4/CC, (C) Co 2p region, (D) Ni 2p region, (E) O 1s region, and (F) B 1s region.
Electrocatalytic nitrate reduction measurements
(A) Schematic illustration of the electrochemical cell and reaction processes.
(B) LSV curves for BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC and NiCo2O4/CC collected in a 0.5 M K2SO4 electrolyte (dotted lines) and a 0.5 M K2SO4 + 200 ppm NO3 --N electrolyte (solid lines).
(C) Potential-dependent FEs of NH4 + and possible by-products over BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC.
(D) Comparison of FEs and yields of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC and NiCo2O4/CC under the same measurement conditions.
(E) FE for nitrate reduction by BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC and other state-of-the-art catalysts.
Isotope-labeling experiment and mechanism investigation
(A) 1H NMR spectra of the electrolyte after electrocatalytic NO3 - reduction reaction on BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC using 15NO3 - and 14NO3 - as the nitrogen source.
(B) In situ Raman spectra of electrocatalytic NO3 - reduction over BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC at different applied potentials.
(C) Free-energy diagrams of NO3 - reduction to NH3 on the surface of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC. Insets are the optimized configurations for adsorbates.
(D) Charge-density difference for NO3 - adsorption on NiCo2O4/CC and BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC. The blue and yellow electronic clouds indicate charge depletion and accumulation, respectively.

head.ce:title: Exclusive nitrate to ammonia conversion via boron-doped carbon dots induced surface Lewis acid sites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green Catalysis Center and College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Waterhouse School of Chemical Sciences, The University of Auckland, Auckland 1142, New Zealand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Waterhouse School of Chemical Sciences, The University of Auckland, Auckland 1142

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrocatalytic nitrate reduction represents a very promising route for ammonia synthesis under ambient conditions. However, for most nitrate-to-ammonia electrocatalysts developed to date, the Faradaic efficiency (FE) to ammonia is modest due to the competitive hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Here, we report that the decoration of vertically aligned NiCo2O4 pillars with boron-doped carbon dots (BCDs) enables electrocatalytic nitrate reduction to ammonia with ∼100% FE. A BCDs/NiCo2O4/carbon cloth cathode delivers a NH3 production rate of 173.9 μmol h−1 cm−2 at −0.55 V versus reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). This state-of-the-art performance is linked to the BCDs, which provide an abundance of Lewis acid sites on the electrocatalyst surface for the adsorption of nitrate ions (simultaneously suppressing the competitive HER). This work reveals that highly selective NH3 electrosynthesis can be achieved under ambient conditions by regulating the surface acidity/basicity properties of metal-oxide-based catalysts, paving the way toward more sustainable NH3-production technologies.

Electrocatalytic nitrate reduction constitutes an alternative and sustainable route for ammonia synthesis under ambient conditions. Lu et al. present that boron-doped carbon dots attached to a vertically aligned NiCo2O4 host enable high Faradaic efficiency of nearly 100% for ammonia electrosynthesis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data supporting the findings of this study are presented within the article and supplemental information. All other data are available from the lead contact upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Ammonia (NH3) is a vitally important commodity chemical in today’s chemical industry. Further, it can serve as a carbon-free clean energy carrier with high energy density (3,000 Wh kg−1) and high hydrogen storage capacity (17.7 wt %), suggesting a key role in the future fuels sector. 1–3 Currently, almost all NH3 is produced via the Haber-Bosch process in which nitrogen and hydrogen gas are reacted over iron-based catalysts at high temperatures (400°C–500°C) and pressures (20–50 MPa). 4
,
5 Owing to the scale of industrial NH3 production and energy-intensive nature of the Haber-Bosch process, it has been estimated that NH3 production comprises about 2% of the world’s total energy consumption per annum. 6
,
7 However, the global nitrogen cycle is unbalanced due to the overuse of nitrogen fertilizers and arbitrary discharge of wastewater. With a view toward sustainable manufacturing and nitrogen cycle, there is a pressing need to discover alternative technologies for NH3 synthesis, ideally technologies that can utilize renewably generated electricity to drive distributed and point-of-use NH3 production under ambient conditions.

Due to the inert nature of N2 (N≡N bonds have a very high dissociation energy of 941 kJ mol−1) and the low solubility of N2 in aqueous solutions, electrocatalytic N2-to-NH3 conversion inevitably results in a poor Faradaic efficiency (FE) and low NH3 yield. 8 In comparison, the dissociation energy for N–O bonds (204 kJ mol−1) in nitrate (NO3 -) is much lower, while the high solubility of NO3 - in aqueous solutions avoids the need for complex gas-liquid-solid three-phase interface catalyst systems used in electrocatalytic N2 reduction. 9 Therefore, using NO3 - as the nitrogen source for electrochemical NH3 synthesis is expected to be hugely beneficial in terms of activity and efficiency. 10 Further, the widespread use of nitrogen-based fertilizers in agriculture, together with industrial discharges, has elevated NO3 - levels in the environment, with the associated eutrophication threatening human health and the stability of fragile ecosystems. 11–13 Electrocatalytic NO3 - reduction to NH3 is therefore additionally attractive as a potential approach for addressing NO3 - pollution and restoring the imbalance in the global nitrogen cycle. 14

The reduction of NO3 - to NH3 requires eight electrons and nine protons (NO3 - + 9H+ + 8e- → NH3 + 3H2O). 15 The complex reaction pathways makes achieving a high FE to NH3 very challenging (cf., 2H+ + 2e- → H2 is a much simpler process). 16 To date, many strategies have been developed to improve the selectivity of NO3 - reduction toward NH3. Yu et al. reported that electron deficiency created by the rectifying Schottky contact in Co/CoO heterostructures could suppress the formation of by-products (including hydrogen evolution reaction [HER]), thus increasing the FE and selectivity toward NH3. 17 In addition, strategies such as single atom, 18 metal alloy, 19–21 and oxygen vacancy 22–24 have been reported, resulting in FEs to NH3 greater than 80%. However, the competitive HER still occurs, resulting in the loss of FE and NH3 yield. Considering that the NO3 --to-NH3 reaction involves many surface adsorption processes, enhancing the adsorption of nitrogen-containing species by adjusting the surface acidity or basicity of a catalyst would appear a logical approach for suppressing the undesirable HER. 25 NO3 - is a weak Lewis base, thus introducing abundant Lewis acid sites on the surface of an electrocatalyst should promote the adsorption of NO3 -. 26 Boron (B) atoms with unoccupied valence 2p orbitals can accept donor electrons and thus serve as Lewis acid sites. 27
,
28 Therefore, the design and synthesis of B-doped electrocatalysts is expected to facilitate the selective NO3 - electrocatalytic reduction to NH3.

Herein, employing a conductive carbon cloth (CC) as a support material, we used a simple hydrothermal reaction and subsequent high-temperature-annealing step to fabricate BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC electrodes for electrocatalytic NO3 --to-NH3 conversion (Figure 1A). The B-doped carbon dots (BCDs) and NiCo2O4 formed vertical nanowire arrays on the CC. The BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC electrocatalyst delivered a remarkable FE toward NH3 (∼100%) at a low overpotential, with undesirable hydrogen generation completely inhibited unlike the reference NiCo2O4/CC catalyst without BCDs. 15N isotope-labeling experiments confirmed that the NH3 produced originated from NO3 - rather than some other source. Under optimized conditions, a NH3 production rate of 173.9 μmol h−1 cm−2 at −0.55 V (versus reversible hydrogen electrode [RHE]) was achieved with the BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC electrocatalyst. The origins of high FE and selectivity and structure-property relations of the state-of-the-art electrocatalyst were systematically explored using a wide range of ex situ techniques, control experiments, in situ Raman spectroscopy, and computational studies, with the Lewis acidity of the BCDs playing a critical role.

BCDs with a mean size ∼3 nm were prepared from 3-aminobenzene borate hydrochloride and possessed a characteristic lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Figure S1A). 29 X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy further confirmed the successful synthesis of BCDs (Figures S1B–S1D). Figure 1A describes the preparation of the BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC nanowire arrays. Specifically, the prepared BCDs, CoCl2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, urea, and clean CC were used to synthesize the BCDs/NiCo(OH)x/CC catalyst precursor catalyst (Figure 1B) via a hydrothermal process. The BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC electrocatalyst was then obtained by heating BCDs/NiCo(OH)x/CC at 450°C for 2 h in air. During the annealing step, the morphology of the nanowire arrays changed from smooth to rough (Figures 1C and S2C–S2D), creating abundant active sites for electrochemical reactions. However, the morphology of NiCo2O4/CC catalyst prepared without BCDs showed little change after annealing (Figures S2A and S2B), indicating that BCDs played an important role in the structural/morphological evolution of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC during annealing.

A scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) image and the corresponding element mapping images for BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC are displayed in Figure 1D. The elements Co, Ni, O, and C were uniformly distributed throughout the catalyst, confirming that the carbon dots were successfully incorporated in the electrode. Owing to the low B-doping level in the BCDs, no obvious signal due to B was seen in the mapping images. In order to confirm the introduction of B and to probe the surface structure of the catalyst, aberration-correction high-angle annular dark-field STEM (AC-HAADF-STEM) was utilized. As shown in Figure 1E, NiCo2O4/CC is highly crystalline with regularly arranged metal atoms. Lattice fringes with spacings of 0.243 and 0.235 nm correspond to the (311) and (222) crystal planes of NiCo2O4, respectively. 30 The presence of BCDs caused reconfiguration of the catalyst, that is, two distinct atomic arrangements on the surface and the interior structures (Figure 1F). To further confirm this phenomenon, we observed similar images in another AC TEM on BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC (Figure 1G). Closer examination of the red boxed area in Figure 1F revealed a six-membered carbon ring framework of BCDs (Figure 1H). Similar results were also found in Figure 1I. Results demonstrate the intimate contact between BCDs and the NiCo2O4 carriers. This interaction was expected to modulate the electron density at the interfaces between the BCDs and NiCo2O4, conferring positive benefits for selective electrocatalytic NO3 - reduction.

The XRD patterns of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC, NiCo2O4/CC, and CC are displayed in Figure 2A. Diffraction peaks centered at 31.2°, 36.7°, 59.1°, and 65.0° in the patterns of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC and NiCo2O4/CC correspond to NiCo2O4 (PDF#73-1702). 31
,
32
Figure 2B shows Raman spectra for the samples. The CC showed characteristic carbon D- and G-band signals at 1,355 and 1,601 cm−1, respectively. 33 After loading NiCo2O4 on the CC, additional signals due to NiCo2O4 appeared in the 400–700 cm−1 region, while the CC signals lost intensity. For BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC, the carbon signals were enhanced (compared with both NiCo2O4/CC and CC), indicating that BCDs were successfully loaded. Further, the positions of the two carbon peaks were at slightly lower position for BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC (1,353 and 1,598 cm−1, respectively), which may have been due to the B doping of CDs or interaction between the BCDs and NiCo2O4.

The near-surface region element composition and surface chemical state of the catalysts were analyzed by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC (Figure S3) revealed the presence of Co, Ni, C, O, and B. The Co 2p spectrum of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC shows three sets of peaks (each with 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 signals in a 2:1 area ratio). Peaks at 781.3 and 796.5 eV are due to Co2+, peaks at 778.6 and 793.7 eV are due to Co3+, and peaks at 802.2 and 787.0 eV are due to shake-up satellites, respectively (Figure 2C). 34 Formally, NiCo2O4 contains Co3+, thus the presence of some Co2+ may have resulted from oxygen vacancies in NiCo2O4 or charge transfer to Ni cations. The Ni 2p XPS spectrum of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC (Figure 2D) also showed three sets of peaks, 855.6/873.1, 853.8/871.1, and 861.4/879.6 eV, all of which are associated with multiplet splitting of Ni2+ in NiCo2O4 (i.e., very similar to Ni2+ in NiO). 35 Compared with data collected for the NiCo2O4/CC catalyst, the presence of BCDs caused the Co 2p signals to shift to higher binding energies (likely by BCDs withdrawing electron density from Co). The C 1s XPS spectrum of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC contained three peaks at 284.6 (C=C/C–C), 286.0 (C–O), and 288.3 eV (C=O) (Figure S4). The O 1s spectrum showed peaks at 529.4, 530.8, and 532.2 eV due to lattice oxygen (i.e. Co–O/Ni–O in NiCo2O4), surface hydroxyl groups, and C=O, respectively. 36 It is worth noting that the characteristic peak attributed to B–O (centered at 531.6 eV) appears in the O 1s spectrum of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC (Figure 2E). As shown in Figures 2F and 2B, 1s spectrum further confirmed the presence of B in several distinct states in the BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC electrode. 37

Electrocatalytic nitrate reduction measurements were conducted under ambient conditions in an H-type cell with a proton exchange membrane (Figure 3A). Both the cathode and anode chambers were filled with 0.5 M K2SO4 electrolyte. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were first collected in the absence of nitrate (Figure 3B, dotted lines). 38 Then, a certain amount of KNO3 was added into the working electrode chamber to achieve a NO3 --N concentration of 200 ppm, after which LSV curves were again collected (Figure 3B, solid lines). Compared with the steady-state LSV curves collected in the absence of nitrate, the current densities of the BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC and NiCo2O4/CC electrodes both showed a significant increase in the presence of 200 ppm NO3 --N at a potential of −0.30 V versus RHE. This indicated that NO3 - participates in the electrocatalytic reaction. Notably, the current density of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC was much higher than that of NiCo2O4/CC, indicating that the introduction of BCDs increased the reaction rate. FEs for nitrate reduction at fixed potentials were tested using the chronoamperometry (CA) method (Figure S5). After the reaction, ion chromatography (IC) was used to quantitatively detect the concentrations of reactants and products, including NO3 -, NO2 -, and NH4 + (Figures S6–S8). The FEs of NH4 + obtained for BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC (Figure 3C) exhibited a volcano-like trend in the potential range −0.35 to −0.75 V versus RHE, with a maximum FE of 100% obtained at −0.55 V. Possible by-products of H2, N2H4, and NO2 - were also detected (Figure S9). When the potential is greater than −0.55 V, the by-product is mainly NO2 -, while when the potential is less than −0.55 V, the by-product is mainly H2. N2H4 was not generated at every applied potential.

To highlight the role of BCDs on BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC in boosting the electrocatalytic NO3 - reduction performance, comparison tests of FE and NH4 + production rates were conducted. As shown in Figure 3D, NiCo2O4/CC shows inferior performance to BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC under the same measurement conditions. At −0.55 V, BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC delivered a NH4 + production rate of 173.9 μmol h−1 cm−2 and a FE of 100%, while NiCo2O4/CC showed a NH4 + production rate of 62.8 μmol h−1 cm−2 and a FE of 58.7%. The reduced FE and NH4 + production rates in the case of NiCo2O4/CC were due to competing HER, since bubbles were observed to form on the surface of NiCo2O4/CC during the tests (no bubbles formed on BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC). In addition, both the electrodes of BCDs/CC and CC show poor performance compared with BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC (Figures S10 and S11).

Electrochemically active surface areas (ECSAs) were next calculated from electrochemical double-layer capacitances (Figure S12). The ECSA of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC (20.18 mF cm−2) was roughly three times higher than that of NiCo2O4/CC (6.86 mF cm−2). Thus, adding B-doped CDs in the fabrication of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC is highly advantageous in terms of increasing the active sites for electrochemistry (this is consistent with the SEM images of NiCo2O4/CC and BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC in Figure 1, where the latter possessed a much rougher morphology). In addition to simple morphological effects, B atoms with an unoccupied 2p orbital in valence shell have weak Lewis acid characteristics and thus will enhance the adsorption of NO3 - and inhibit H adsorption/HER on the surface of catalyst, resulting in both the excellent activity and selectivity for NH3 generation. We compared the FE of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC with various other catalysts reported recently in the literature (Figure 3E; Table S1). Most catalysts have FEs in the range 20%–90%, with neither noble metal nor transition metal catalysts able to completely inhibit HER as BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC. Subsequently, the stability of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC for electrocatalytic nitrate reduction was investigated. The FEs to NH3 remained around 100% and the current density constant during the five consecutive recycling tests (Figures S13 and S14). In a long-term stability test, the formation rate of NH4 + is fast at high NO3 - concentrations. With the consumption of NO3 -, the rate of NH4 + formation decreases gradually (Figure S15). Our work conclusively demonstrates that by using BCDs to modulate the surface Lewis acidity, electrocatalytic NO3 - reduction to NH3 with ∼100% FE is achievable.

To verify that the NH3 generated arose from electrocatalytic NO3 - reduction reaction on BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC rather than some other source (i.e., contaminants), 15N isotopic labeling experiments were conducted. Experiments were conducted using either K15NO3 or K14NO3 as reactants into 0.5 M K2SO4 electrolyte solutions. When 15NO3 - was used, the 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR; 600 MHz) spectrum (Figure 4A) showed two peaks due to 15NH4 +. When 14NO3 - was used, the 1H NMR spectrum showed three peaks typical for 14NH4 +. 24 These experiments confirmed that BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC was active for the electrocatalytic NO3 - reduction, excluding possible interference from environmental contaminants or the electrocatalyst itself.

To gain better understanding of the relationship between surface Lewis acid sites and nitrate reduction activity, we performed in situ Raman measurements on BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC at different applied potentials (Figure 4B). The sharp peak at 975 cm−1 is assigned to a symmetric N–O stretching vibration peak of NO3 - adsorbed on the catalyst surface, while the broad peaks near 3,230 and 3,440 cm−1 are assigned to O–H stretching vibrations of adsorbed hydroxyl groups. The intensity of the 975 cm−1 peak increased with the applied potential, implying that the Lewis acid sites created by the BCDs played a key role in increasing adsorption capacity for NO3 -. The signal due to adsorbed hydroxyl first intensified with increasing potential up to −0.50 V versus RHE (near the optimal potential for nitrate reduction to NH3 in Figure 3C) then lost intensity at potentials of −0.65 V or higher (due to the onset of HER). Results are consistent with NO3 - reduction to NH3 consuming protons, with the presence of optimal amounts of surface adsorbed nitrate and hydroxyl groups (proton source) enabling very efficient NH3 production. 39

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were used to further explore the origin of the exceptional catalytic activity of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC for nitrate reduction. Bader charge analysis results show that the B atoms transfers 1.74 and 1.83 electrons to the surrounding C atoms, respectively, and the empty orbital of the B atoms can form a Lewis acid center (Figure S16). Based on the calculated adsorption free energies of adsorbates (Table S2), a favorable Lewis-acid-site-guided reaction pathway was identified (Figures 4C and S17). The charge-density difference for NO3 - on NiCo2O4/CC and BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC is depicted in Figure 4D, allowing exploration of the role of B in modifying the surface electronic structure of the catalysts. A larger electron cloud was observed between NO3 - and metal atoms on BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC than between NO3 - and metal atoms on NiCo2O4/CC. This suggests a stronger interaction between NO3 - and metal atoms in the case of BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC, consistent with the calculated NO3 - adsorption energies of −3.26 and −2.68 eV on BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC and NiCo2O4/CC, respectively. In addition, the adsorption energies of H+ and NO3 - on the surface of NiCo2O4/CC and BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC were calculated, indicating that both NiCo2O4/CC and BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC have a good inhibitory effect on HER in the nitrate solution (Figure S18). No N2 product was detected experimentally, which agrees well with our DFT calculations that the free energy of continuous hydrogenation of ∗N intermediate is less than that coupling ∗N intermediates to form N2 (Figure S19). Based on our experimental and computational findings, a plausible reaction mechanism for electrocatalytic NO3 - reduction for NH3 synthesis on BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC could be deduced. Without BCDs on the catalyst, the reaction sites on NiCo2O4/CC surface have limited absorption capacity for NO3 -, resulting in a lot of atomic hydrogen adsorption on the catalyst surface to promote the HER. The introduction of BCDs creates a large number of Lewis acid sites that promote the adsorption of NO3 -, thus facilitating the NO3 --to-NH3 conversion reaction and completely inhibiting hydrogen evolution, resulting in a 100% FE for NH3 synthesis.

In conclusion, a BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC cathode is reported capable of efficiently driving electrocatalytic nitrate reduction under ambient conditions with ∼100% FE to NH3. By utilizing a series of in situ and ex situ techniques and DFT calculations, it demonstrates that the abundant surface Lewis acid sites in BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC created through the introduction of BCDs enhance nitrate adsorption while suppressing HER, thereby allowing highly selective NH3 synthesis from NO3 -. To our knowledge, BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC is the first reported catalyst to completely suppress HER during electrocatalytic nitrate reduction. The simple catalyst design strategy introduced in this work (i.e., catalyst modification with heteroatom-doped CDs) could potentially be utilized in a wide range of electrocatalytic reactions, allowing precise tuning of catalyst surface acidity and basicity for improved reaction efficiency and suppression of undesirable side reactions. Results encourage further exploration of the potential of doped carbon dots in electrocatalysis.
Further information and requests for resources and reagents should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Siyu Lu (sylu2013@zzu.edu.cn).
This study did not generate new unique reagents.

All reagents were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China) and used without further purification. K15NO3 (99 atom% 15N, purity >98.5%) and 15NH4Cl (99 atom% 15N, purity >98.5%) were used for the isotope-labeling experiments.

1.0 mmol CoCl2·6H2O, 2.0 mmol NiCl2·6H2O, 0.3 g BCDs, and 15 mmol urea were added to 30 mL of ultrapure water and stirred at 500 RPM for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, after which a piece of clean CC (3 × 2 cm2) was placed in the solution. The autoclave was then sealed and heated at 120°C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the modified CC was washed with ultrapure water and ethanol 3 times, then dried at 60°C under vacuum to obtain the catalyst precursor BCDs/NiCo(OH)x/CC. Finally, BCDs/NiCo2O4/CC was obtained by heating BCDs/NiCo(OH)x/CC in air at 450°C.
For comparison, a NiCo2O4/CC electrocatalyst was prepared in the same manner without adding BCDs in the electrode-fabrication process.

Electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China). The reactor comprised a typical H-type electrolytic cell with a Nafion 117 membrane. Both the cathode and anode compartments used 50 mL of 0.5 M K2SO4 solution as the electrolyte. KNO3 was added into the cathode compartment to achieve a NO3 --N concentration of 200 ppm. The area of the working electrode immersed in the electrolyte was 1 cm2, and the current density was normalized by the geometric surface area. LSV data were collected at a scanning rate of 5 mV s−1 over the potential range −0.05 to −0.75 V versus RHE. To calculate the FE, potentiostatic tests were performed at different applied potentials (−0.35, −0.45, −0.55, −0.65, and −0.75 V versus RHE) for 7,200 s. Stability tests were performed using the same working electrode while continuously replenishing the electrolyte and reactants. CV curves used in determination of the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) were measured in a potential window without any Faradaic processes at scan rates of 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mV s−1. The resulting plots of current density (at a set potential) against scan rate showed a linear relationship with slope equal to the Cdl.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52122308, 21905253, and 51973200), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M682332 and 2021T140614), and the Henan Postdoctoral Foundation (202003015).

ce:section.ce:para: X.L., Q.Z., J.C., S.Y., and L.G. contributed to data collecting. J.Y. contributed to theoretical calculation. X.L., J.C., and G.I.N.W. contributed to date analyzing and manuscript writing. S.-Q.Z. and B.Y. contributed to manuscript revision. J.C. and S.L. contributed to project design. S.L. supervised the project.
The authors declare no competing interests.
Supplemental information can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.xcrp.2022.100961.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Document S1. Figures S1–S19, Tables S1 and S2, and supplemental experimental procedures
Document S2. Article plus supplemental information
